BrewingEnhanced.BeerStyleDef.Label: Off Beer
BrewingEnhanced.BeerStyleDef.TastingNotes: Beer that hasn't properly fermented, probably due to incorrect temperature. Yuck!

BrewingEnhanced.BeerStyleDef.Label: Off Beer
BrewingEnhanced.BeerStyleDef.TastingNotes: Yuck... did someone leave the fermenter lid open? Why would they do that? This beer needs more wheat for this to work.

BrewingEnhanced.BeerStyleDef.Name: BeerStyle_Ale
BrewingEnhanced.BeerStyleDef.Label: Craft Ale
BrewingEnhanced.BeerStyleDef.TastingNotes: A unique ale, that could only be brewed on the Rim. It's got a fine balance of malts and hops, but it's difficult to pin down the exact flavour. Pawns smile at you and tell you it's great, although you can't quite tell if they are just being polite. You think it tastes nice.

BrewingEnhanced.BeerStyleDef.Label: Ale
BrewingEnhanced.BeerStyleDef.TastingNotes: A great balance of malt and hop flavours, topped off with a unique fruity bouquet that slices through the bitterness. Bartender. Bring me another!

BrewingEnhanced.BeerStyleDef.Label: Mexican Craft Beer
BrewingEnhanced.BeerStyleDef.TastingNotes: A light, refreshing ale fermented with agave nectar, giving a clean, crisp body with subtle honey-like sweetness. Notice the soft, floral notes.

BrewingEnhanced.BeerStyleDef.Name: BeerStyle_PaleAle
BrewingEnhanced.BeerStyleDef.Label: Pale Ale
BrewingEnhanced.BeerStyleDef.TastingNotes: A hop-forward ale brewed with lightly roasted barley malts. A fine combination of crisp and caramel flavours giving it a well balanced body. The balance pairs wonderfully with the bitterness from the hops.

BrewingEnhanced.BeerStyleDef.Label: Double IPA
BrewingEnhanced.BeerStyleDef.TastingNotes: IPAs were rumoured to be invented by the British, to get beer out to troops in India. They added more hops to the beer to better preserve it for the long sea journey. It just so happened to taste wonderful. Modern beers add even more hops to make it a "Double IPA", to get even more of that unique hoppy flavour into the final product.

BrewingEnhanced.BeerStyleDef.Label: Hefeweizen
BrewingEnhanced.BeerStyleDef.TastingNotes: The wheat has caused the beer to become cloudy. It's also resulted in a lighter body and a smooth mouthfeel, with subtle fruity or spicy notes. Combines well with a hot, cloudless summer day. The higher fermenting temperatures can bring out the fruity flavours even more.

BrewingEnhanced.BeerStyleDef.Label: Cream Ale
BrewingEnhanced.BeerStyleDef.TastingNotes: A light, crisp ale with a smooth, mildly sweet body. Its cloudier than normal and comes out the bottle with a deliciously thick head, giving its trademark name.

BrewingEnhanced.BeerStyleDef.Label: Oatmeal Pale Ale
BrewingEnhanced.BeerStyleDef.TastingNotes: A smooth, slightly creamy body that balances citrusy or piney hop characters, depending on the exact oats used. Expect a low malt sweetness and low bitterness.

BrewingEnhanced.BeerStyleDef.Label: Stout
BrewingEnhanced.BeerStyleDef.TastingNotes: A dark ale, almost pitch black, which is a result of roasting the barley malts. The result is a rich flavour, with strong notes of coffee and chocolate. There is a distinct bitter finish on the palette. Enjoyed worldwide for its velvety mouthfeel.

BrewingEnhanced.BeerStyleDef.Label: Berliner Weisse
BrewingEnhanced.BeerStyleDef.TastingNotes: An intentionally tart, acidic beer, brewed by directly encouraging bacteria to interfere with the process. The sweetness of the wheat has expertly counteracted this flavour, however. The result is a crisp, refreshing taste with varying levels of funk, fruitiness, and sourness.

BrewingEnhanced.BeerStyleDef.Label: Fruit Lambic
BrewingEnhanced.BeerStyleDef.TastingNotes: A traditional Belgian sour beer that. Fermented with wild yeast and bacteria, then aged with fruit to develop complex tart, funky, and fruity flavors. The whole process feels like it shouldn't have worked... and yet here we are! Still classified as a beer due to the process and a positive unique flavour you won't forget soon.
